  // Helper function to read a cookie value
  function getCookie(name) {
    var value = "; " + document.cookie;
    var parts = value.split("; " + name + "=");
    if (parts.length == 2) return parts.pop().split(";").shift();
  }

  // Helper function to set a cookie
  function setCookie(name, value, days) {
    var expires = "";
    if (days) {
      var date = new Date();
      date.setTime(date.getTime() + (days*24*60*60*1000));
      expires = "; expires=" + date.toUTCString();
    }
    document.cookie = name + "=" + (value || "")  + expires + "; path=/";
  }

  // Function to update display eligibility based on the time gap
  function updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds) {
    setTimeout(function() {
      window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
      console.log('updateDisplayEligibility - OptinMonster is now display eligible');
    }, waitTimeSeconds * 1000); // Convert seconds to milliseconds
  }

  document.addEventListener('om.Campaign.show', function(event) {
    // console.log('om.Campaign.show', event.detail.Campaign);

    var now = new Date().getTime();
    setCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp', now, 1); // Store timestamp in cookie for 1 day
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';

    updateDisplayEligibility(minimumDisplayGap);
  });

  var now = new Date().getTime();
  var lastDisplayTime = parseInt(getCookie('optinMonsterLastDisplayTimestamp')) || 0;
  var timePassed = (now - lastDisplayTime) / 1000; // Convert milliseconds to seconds

  if (timePassed >= minimumDisplayGap) {
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'true';
    console.log('OptinMonster display eligible on page load');
  } else {
    var waitTimeSeconds = minimumDisplayGap - timePassed;
    window.optinMonsterDisplayEligible = 'false';
    updateDisplayEligibility(waitTimeSeconds);
  }</script> <style id="uagb-style-conditional-extension">@media (min-width: 1025px){body .uag-hide-desktop.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-desktop{display:none !important}}@media (min-width: 768px) and (max-width: 1024px){body .uag-hide-tab.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-tab{display:none !important}}@media (max-width: 767px){body .uag-hide-mob.uagb-google-map__wrap,body .uag-hide-mob{display:none !important}}</style><style id="uagb-style-frontend-62682">.uagb-heading-text mark {

class="site-main"><article class="post-33905 post type-post status-publish format-standard has-post-thumbnail hentry category-diet-set-up category-nutrition ast-article-single" id="post-33905" itemtype="https://schema.org/CreativeWork" itemscope="itemscope"><div class="ast-post-format- single-layout-1 ast-no-date-box"><div class="entry-content clear" itemprop="text"><div id="modal-ready"><p class="has-text-align-center"><strong>#1 <a href="https://rippedbody.com/calories/">Calories</a>, #2 <a href="https://rippedbody.com/macros/">Macros</a>, #3 Micros, #4 <a href="https://rippedbody.com/nutrient-timing/">Nutrient Timing</a>, #5 <a href="https://rippedbody.com/supplements/">Supplements</a></strong></p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img decoding="async" width="1024" height="933" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-1024x933.png" alt="Nutrition Pyramid Micronutrition" class="wp-image-59047" style="width:768px;height:700px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-1024x933.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-450x410.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-768x700.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-1536x1400.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-1568x1429.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/Nutrition-Pyramid-Micros-450x410@2x.png 900w" sizes="(max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"></figure><p class="has-text-align-center">This is the third chapter in my five-part guide on nutrition for fat loss and muscle growth. <strong>Join 100,000 people and <a href="https://rippedbody.com/free-nutrition-course/">download the full PDF version here</a>.</strong></p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity is-style-wide"><p>Here are the sections of this chapter:</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li><a href="#micros">Why Micronutrients Are Important</a></li><li><a href="#fruit-veg">Fruit and Vegetable Intake Guidelines</a></li><li><a href="#deficiencies">Why Dieters Are At Risk of Micronutrient Deficiencies</a></li><li><a href="#water">Water Intake Guidelines</a></li></ul><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h2 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="micros">WHy MICRONUTRIENTS are important</h2><p>Micronutrients are generally counted in milligrams (or less).</p><p>Think of macros as being the gas in your car, giving it the energy to propel the engine; micros as the oil and lubricants, keeping the car from breaking down.</p><p>Long-term micronutrient deficiencies will impact your health and sabotage your training efforts. But, by observing a few simple rules of thumb regarding your daily fruit and vegetable intake, you minimize your risk of deficiencies.</p><p>The most commonly known micronutrients are the <em>vitamins</em> and <em>minerals</em> that we get from the foods we eat, which we can’t live without.</p><p>Other compounds — like <em>phytonutrient</em>s and <em>zoonutrients</em> — aren’t considered vitamins or minerals, but can optimize health and prevent disease. They are obtained from the plant and animal foods we eat. We haven’t figured out how to put these in a pill yet, so a daily multivitamin and mineral can <em>not</em> be considered a substitute for a poor diet, only an insurance policy on a good one.</p><p><strong>Vitamins are organic and come from once-living things. </strong>The fat-soluble ones (A, D, E, and K) are absorbed in the gut, so deficiencies or surpluses (overdoses) build over time. The water-soluble ones are hard to overdose on because they will be passed in the urine. The flip side of this is that they need to be consumed daily.</p><p><strong>Minerals are non-organic.</strong> Some of these (calcium, sodium, potassium, magnesium, etc.) are needed in greater quantities than others (iron, copper, zinc, etc.). Not coincidentally, things containing the former group (dairy & salty foods for example) taste especially good to us.</p><ul class="wp-block-list"><li>Zinc deficiencies can negatively impact your metabolism.</li><li>Iron deficiencies can negatively impact strength.</li><li>Calcium deficiencies can negatively impact bone health.</li></ul><p><strong>There are performance benefits from eating vegetables.</strong> Green vegetables (spinach, rocket, and beetroot in particular) have a lot of nitrates. An increase in nitrate intake can elevate plasma nitrate concentration, which can increase the amount of oxygen supplied to muscle tissue, reducing the cost of exercise and improving exercise tolerance.</p><p>It’s not a night and day difference, but Popeye was onto something with eating all that spinach, and your mum was right in telling you to eat your vegetables.</p><figure class="wp-block-image aligncenter size-large is-resized"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" width="1024" height="539" src="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-1024x539.png" alt="" class="wp-image-59098" style="width:768px;height:404px" srcset="https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-1024x539.png 1024w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-450x237.png 450w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-768x404.png 768w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-1536x808.png 1536w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-2048x1078.png 2048w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-1568x825.png 1568w, https://rippedbody.com/wp-content/uploads/There-are-no-micronutrition-shortcuts-450x237@2x.png 900w" sizes="auto, (max-width: 1024px) 100vw, 1024px"><figcaption class="wp-element-caption">Shredded Sam is pissed. He’s caught Natalie, Freddie, and Thelma trying to take micronutritional shortcuts, despite his warnings that there are none. Time to school them…</figcaption></figure><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="fruit-veg">FRUIT AND VEGetable INTAKE GUIDELINES</h3><p>The majority of people reading this will be fine for the micronutrients we get in meat, dairy, and starchy carbs. <strong>It’s</strong> <strong>generally the ones from fruit and veg that clients typically need to pay attention to</strong>, as they are the foods most often skipped.</p><p>A good starting point is to eat a minimum of two pieces of fruit a day, to make sure you eat a fist-sized portion of fibrous vegetables with every meal, and to vary their intake day-to-day.</p><p>However, it’s a good idea to scale your intake based on how much you are eating overall, so I recommend the following for fruit and veg intake based on calorie intake:</p><h6 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">Fruit and Vegetable Intake Recommendations Based On Calorie Intake</h6><figure class="wp-block-table is-style-stripes"><table><tbody><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center"><strong>CALORIE INTAKE</strong></td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center"><strong>CUPS OF FRUIT & VEG. EACH DAY</strong></td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">1200–2000</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">2 cups each</td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">2000–3000</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">3 cups each</td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">3000–4000</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">4 cups each</td></tr></tbody></table></figure><p>This uses the US cup measurement system, which is ~250 ml, or the size of a typical coffee mug.</p><h4 class="wp-block-heading">Fiber Intake Guidelines</h4><p>Fiber is important for gut health and nutrient absorption.</p><p>Both too little and too much fiber can be detrimental, but if you’re following the guidelines mentioned and not doing anything unusual with the rest of your diet, you’re probably fine.</p><p><img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="👉" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f449.svg"> Current recommendations for fiber intake are 14 g/1000 calories.</p><p>Constipation can be a sign that you’re eating too little; loose stools are a sign that you’re eating too much. Adjust accordingly.</p><p>If you eat a lot of beans, oats, or high-fiber cereal to hit your carbohydrate numbers, your fiber intake is likely too high. You’d have to consume an extraordinary amount of vegetables for this to be the case. See the comparison table below.</p><h6 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">Fiber Content of Fibrous Vegetables vs. Oats and Cereals</h6><figure class="wp-block-table"><table><tbody><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center"><strong>FOODS HIGH IN FIBER</strong></td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center"><strong>FIBER PER 100 g</strong></td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">Asparagus, Broccoli, Cabbage, Cauliflower, Celery, <br>Eggplant, Kale, Lettuce, Onion, Spinach, Zucchini</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">1.3–2.9 g</td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">Beans</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">~5.1 g</td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center"><em>Bran Flakes</em></td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">18 g</td></tr><tr><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">Oats</td><td class="has-text-align-center" data-align="center">11 g</td></tr></tbody></table></figure><figure class="wp-block-image size-large"><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" Sam schools Nat, Fred, and Thel.</figcaption></figure><p>Eat your vegetables. The fiber helps you poop, they keep you full, they have micronutrients we can’t put into a pill or powder, and there are potential performance benefits.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity is-style-wide"><h4 class="wp-block-heading" id="deficiencies">Why <strong>Dieters Are At Risk Of Micronutrient Deficiencies</strong></h4><p>As calories and macronutrients get lower, it becomes harder to prevent micronutrient deficiencies.</p><p>Try to maintain dairy and red meat intake (lean, with the fat trimmed, can fit into almost any diet), and get regular outside sun exposure (not through windows). This should help you avoid calcium, zinc, magnesium, iron, and vitamin D deficiencies. For those who don’t eat meat or dairy, I’ll cover supplements that may be worth considering in that section.</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-css-opacity"><h3 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center" id="water">WATER INTAKE GUIDELINES</h3><p>Water is important for fat loss and performance. I don’t like the idea of setting water intake for people based on body weight, simply because some people sweat more than others, not to mention different climates and activity levels. Therefore, I suggest the following guidelines:</p><ol class="wp-block-list"><li>Aim to be peeing clear by noon.</li><li>Have five clear urinations a day.</li><li>Make sure that<strong> you’re not dehydrated at the time of your workouts</strong> or they will be negatively impacted.</li><li>Taper water intake toward the end of the day as needed so you don’t have to wake to pee.</li></ol><p><br><strong>Thank you for reading. Got a question? Hit me up in the comments.</strong></p><p>– Andy</p><hr class="wp-block-separator has-alpha-channel-opacity is-style-wide"><h4 class="wp-block-heading has-text-align-center">DOWNLOAD THE FULL VERSION itemprop="name">Andy Morgan</h4> </a><div class="post-author-desc">Hi! The Ripped Body® Method is an evidence-based, data-driven approach to physique transformation.
⠀
For the majority of my 20s, I fell for the scams of the fitness industry. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="🤦🏻‍♂️" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f926-1f3fb-200d-2642-fe0f.svg">
⠀
This site is my revenge. <img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="😬" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f62c.svg">
⠀
Since 2011, I’ve written guides explaining how we get results to help readers avoid the same path of frustration. We’ve helped over 2,000 busy working guys transform their bodies WITHOUT supplements, fad diets, or the process dominating their lives.
⠀
<img draggable="false" role="img" class="emoji" alt="📩" src="https://s.w.org/images/core/emoji/17.0.2/svg/1f4e9.svg"> Enter your email address in the box at the bottom of this page and I'd be honored to show you how.</div></div></div></div></div><div class="wpdiscuz_top_clearing"></div><div id="comments" class="comments-area"><div id="respond" style="width: 0;height: 0;clear: both;margin: 0;padding: 0;"></div><div id="wpdcom" class="wpdiscuz_unauth wpd-default wpd-layout-1 wpd-comments-open"><div class="wc_social_plugin_wrapper"></div><div class="wpd-form-wrap"><div class="wpd-form-head"><div class="wpd-auth"><div class="wpd-login"></div></div></div><div class="wpd-form wpd-form-wrapper wpd-main-form-wrapper" id="wpd-main-form-wrapper-0_0"><form method="post" enctype="multipart/form-data" data-uploading="false" class="wpd_comm_form wpd_main_comm_form"><p style="font-size:20px; font-weight:900; text-transform:uppercase; margin-bottom:10px; margin-top:40px;">Got a question?     <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hey Andy! i did carb back-loading year and half ago and completely eliminated fruits and dairy from my diet. i heard or read somewhere during cbl that fructose and dairy is bad and makes you fat. And result of avoiding fruits and dairy is that i have got calcium deficiency, cant get uninterrupted sleep, have to wake up couple of times during night, getting bald and my hair have become so thin from top. i always take multivitamin with food but it doesn’t seem to be working. I have introduced fruits and dairy again in my diet. cottage cheese and berries for dinner is a staple and have started taking magnesium tablets for sleep. Sleep has become better but not perfect. Cant find any solution for hair regrowth so far. i eat bananas, apples,grapes and oranges couple of times/ week. Hope you could help me with my micro nutrient deficiency</p>

</div>

    <div class="wpd-comment-text">
    <p>Hi Chiranjeev.<br>
What you have read is complete nonsense. Sorry to hear that it had such affects. You probably just need to give it time for your hair to come back, however I’m not knowledgable on this kind of thing. If anything sounds too good to be true, it is. From the opening paragraphs of the <a href="https://rippedbody.com/nutritional-hierarchy-importance-4-meal-timing-frequency-macro-cycling/" title="The Nutritional Hierarchy of Importance – #4 Meal Timing & Frequency, Calorie & Macro Cycling" rel="nofollow">fourth article on timing</a> in this series:</p>
<p><em>It appeals to us that something as simple as changing the timing of things can have a potent effect. People go mad for any short cut to actually putting in some effort and marketeers take advantage of this (flash a little bit of science while conveniently not talking about the bigger picture) to sell us on something new. Any time someone presents you the nutritional importance pyramid upside-down your BS detector should go off.</em></p><em>
</em><p><em>The truth: Getting the timing of things right most certainly has favourable effects on body composition, however, if you gloss over the most impactful, foundation levels of your nutrition plan (calorie intake, the macro composition, and the micronutrition) you are wasting your time, money and effort.</em></p>

</div>

  // Slug arrays
  var trainingSegmentSlugs = ['training']; // Add more training slugs as needed
  var nutritionSegmentSlugs = ['nutrition']; // Add more nutrition slugs as needed

  // Function to check if any slug exists in body class
  function segmentSlugExists(slugArray) {
    return slugArray.some(function(slug) {
      return bodyClassList.contains('category-' + slug);

  }

metadata.description: Long-term micronutrient deficiencies will torpedo your training efforts. Follow these guidelines on the foods to eat to minimize your risk of deficiencies.
